content.problemStatement:
Salmon is playing a game!

He is given two integers NNN and KKK. His goal is to output KKK pairs (xi,yi)(x_i,y_i)(xi​,yi​). He creates a function fff such that f(xi)=yif(x_i) = y_if(xi​)=yi​ and fff is undefined for all other values of xxx. His pairs must then meet the following conditions:


All xix_ixi​ are distinct.
All yiy_iyi​ are distinct.
All xi,yix_i, y_ixi​,yi​ are in the range [0,2N−1][0,2^N - 1][0,2N−1] and are integers.
f(f(xi))f(f(x_i))f(f(xi​)) is defined for all iii.
Let H(x)H(x)H(x) be the number of set bits in the binary representation of xxx. Then, H(xi)≠H(f(f(xi)))H(x_i) \neq H(f(f(x_i)))H(xi​)=H(f(f(xi​))) for all iii.
Unfortunately, Salmon is unable to solve this. Help Salmon win the game!

If there are multiple correct solutions, you may output any one of them. It can be proven that a solution exists for all NNN and KKK that meet constraints.
constraints: The sum of 2N2^N2N over all testcases doesn't exceed 2182^{18}218

`ExplanationIn the answer to the first testcase, there are 7 pairs. xix_ixi​ and yiy_iyi​ are all in the range [0,23−1][0,2^3 - 1][0,23−1], which is the range [0,7][0,7][0,7]. All values of xix_ixi​ are distinct, and all values of yiy_iyi​ are distinct. However, note that some integers appear in both lists. For example, 5 appears both as x1x_1x1​ and as y4y_4y4​. This is allowed. For all xix_ixi​, f(f(xi))f(f(x_i))f(f(xi​)) is defined and H(f(f(xi)))≠H(xi)H(f(f(x_i))) \neq H(x_i)H(f(f(xi​)))=H(xi​). For example, f(f(7))=4f(f(7)) = 4f(f(7))=4. H(7)=3H(7) = 3H(7)=3, but H(4)=1H(4) = 1H(4)=1. Therefore, H(f(f(7)))≠H(7)H(f(f(7))) \neq H(7)H(f(f(7)))=H(7), as desired.
content.inputFormat:
The first line input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of testcases
Each of the next TTT lines contains two space-separated integers NNN and KKK respectively.
content.outputFormat:
Output KKK lines for each testcase.
The iii-th line of a testcase should contain two space-separated integers: xix_ixi​ and yiy_iyi​, following the given constraints.
